title: Is “Vibe Coding” Going to Make a Generation of Lazy Programmers?
author: Erik Ralston
text:
Is “Vibe Coding” Going to Make a Generation of Lazy Programmers?
Ruminations on the Role of AI in Software Careers
This post is a summary of a talk originally deliver in June 2025 to hopefully shift the mindset of folks on AI-assisted coding. I believe it’s important to both open you mind to it as a skill to write code faster while realizing it’s must ONLY a way to write code faster and not (yet) a replacement for deep engineering skills. It is also revisiting the topic of When AI Finally Writes All the Code, What Will Us Developers Do? I first wrote about in 2023.
Much has been made since February about the practice of “Vibe Coding”, or the full embrace of AI coding tools as not just a way of writing code faster, but as a new medium for expressing one’s intent when building products. What is Vibe Coding? A term for AI-assisted coding coined by Andrej Karpathy, co-founder of OpenAI.
Since the release of Replit and Cursor, I had been hearing about its primordial forms; however, the hype was unbearable. I was suffering a motivation block to try it due to pondering the potential continuum of realities that we’re either in or about to be in:
- The tools work flawlessly. Am I out of a job?
- The tools work, but not flawlessly. Does my job transform into a code plumber that debugs incomprehensible nonsense remixed from GitHub?
- The tools barely work, but the industry adopts them so widely that you must suffer their boundless mediocrity. Is this just the next JavaScript?
- The tools do not work, but I’m forced to use them anyway. Does manager consensus in business news derail my career?
- The tools do NOT work, and this is a temporary fad in the industry that falls apart within a year. Does that mean learning skills now and I throw them away later like a USB drive full of NFT private keys?
This rumination led to analysis paralysis until I realized that the extreme outcomes on either end of that spectrum mean I should stop learning entirely—the ultimate absurdity. The arrival of GitHub Copilot Agent Mode put the potential for it right into my IDE. I’ve been using GitHub Copilot in my day-to-day work for years at this point, so when the time and opportunity presented themselves, I took the chance to dive deeper.
In summary, I learned a lot. From stepping up from “autocomplete on steroids” into fully conversational project changes to complete vibe coding on Bolt.New to understanding the highest velocity vibes while using the highest level abstractions. Some lessons are timeless, while others are temporary. Ultimately, I believe “vibe coding” to some extent is the future, but most of all, because it’s just the past brought to new life, and another tool to make the best of us even better when it comes to software engineering. However, it can also be a trap for beginners, allowing them to develop bad habits more quickly than ever. Why?
Will AI Coding Make Us Lazy?
Twenty years ago, while studying Computer Science at Washington State University, I sat down for one of the last “filter classes” in the program, taught by Professor Kung-Chi Wang. Professor Wang taught a two-part sequence of Operating Systems classes, which the other students warned me were nearly impossible for those who were not on track to graduate. Professor Wang would gave us some of the most challening and mind-expanding work in my time, culminating inbuilding our rendition of a POSIX-like operating system that ran on a single 3.5" floppy disk.
When ChatGPT first arrived, memories of Professor Wang’s class were the trigger for my first “holy crap” moment making code with it. One of the main challenges in his first semester was writing a boot loader — a program that loads the operating system into memory and starts it on the PC — so remembering his class, I asked ChatGPT to write one. With one prompt, it spat back out a C program very similar to what I made. Weeks of learning for me were recreated in ten seconds or less.
The most notable aspect of operating system programming, especially given the tiny memory constraints of a boot loader, is that it’s a program that one likely can’t write in just C code. C is a so-called “high-level language”. While I had done a class on Assembly programming, the boot loader was the first time I had ever worked with adding inline assembly code into my C. The purpose of the class was to force you to think not only beneath the level of an application and into the architecture of an operating system, but also from the language of the human to the reality of the silicon.
He was also very clear about his inner monologue when writing the code. He liked to talk about how programming is an art form for the lazy because if he wanted to work hard, he’d use a shovel, not a keyboard. Almost every time he wrote another loop, mentioned the importance of code reuse, or pointed out a technique for minimizing instructions sent to the processor, he would say his favorite quote that captured the paradox of being a programmer:
Although I’ve never had to write assembly code again in my career, I've internalized this as a perennial lesson from his classes. My coworkers have heard it, my children have listened to it to death, and his voice whispers it in my mind every time I search for an npm package. It captures the essence of how programmers must understand every level of the behavior they want in their system, but must pursue creating their answer with the least amount of code and computational resources required to solve the problem.
Which brings us back around to the moral hazard of AI programming:
It’s the latest in laziness.
Even Professor Wang probably wouldn’t say that’s a bad thing. It’s just another form of technology as a double-edged sword. It has always been, and will continue to be, true that the highest level of achievement requires the deepest level of understanding. Any content online claiming otherwise was likely authored by the deceptive who haven’t done anything or the lucky who only accomplished something by accident. As AI begins to impact creative work, both individuals and society are standing at a crossroads. Turning the machines off is not a viable option, so we need to learn how to utilize them to create a sustainable society and career.
As programmers, it should be evident to us that AI Tools themselves are built upon abstractions that are intended to hide complexity, but that shouldn’t stop us from being curious about that complexity, nor alleviate our duty to examine those depths — if only for a semester of our careers — so we understand the world top-down and bottom-up. He was an Electrical Engineer by education, so he started at the bottom rung of computer science, understanding the hardware, and could climb up to the top.
The fact that I only contemplated NAND and XOR gates in electronics for a week in 2004 and never again makes me feel a sense of pride in my laziness. I arrived just as jQuery and its ecosystem of libraries claimed to shrink the amount of JavaScript people needed to understand and write. Yet it continued into an era where people copy and paste yet more JavaScript from Stack Overflow to solve problems. Am I already the lazy generation that AI will bring about?
If you’re a developer learning AI tools, even if the hierarchy above is an imperfect metaphor, you should see them as the latest level of abstraction. You are still obligated to learn and think in your work, top-to-bottom and bottom-to-top, across the entire hierarchy of understanding, but that has been true since the beginning. Unless you're working in a codebase where you write assembly, you’re already standing on a tightrope held up by scaffolding that we barely think about. Contrary to popular advice, in this balancing act, you must look down from time to time to understand where your next step takes you, and AI raises this tightrope even higher. We must still avoid the mistake of thinking it’s the same as knowing how to fly.
The real danger is that we each have only so many keystrokes in our lives, and most of us have more ideas than we can ever build in that modest allotment. It’s not that we’re lazy, it’s that we’re limited in so many other fundamental ways — from how much our mind can think at once to how much time on the keyboard we have in a week — that we need to push boundaries, and AI is the latest means to do it—time to explore the right way to stay smart by being lazy.
UPDATE: This post happened to coincide with a news cycle around how MIT research around brain scans of ChatGPT users and their real behavior in essay writing eroded critical thinking over time. I take it as validation to the concepts in this article and how we are at an ethical and intellectual crossroads at least at the individual level. I do think introducing LLMs to the learning process is broadly dangerous because it will make people who start out lazy and never become smart. How do we prevent that? An article for another time.
If you want to read more on this topic of what AI tools mean for making software, read more in my series: What AI Tools Mean for Software Projects
Erik Ralston graduated from Washington State University with a degree in Computer Science after many late nights finally turning Professor Wang’s whiteboard wisdom into real code. He is an innovator with 19 years of experience in the industry. Erik is also co-founder of Fuse Accelerator in the emerging community of Tri-Cities, WA, where he works on connecting people and sharing knowledge to turn new ideas into growing startups. You can find him on LinkedIn or the next Fuse event.
